content.assessment: Delaying selection to age 14 directly targets the institutional feature most implicated by the lens findings: an unusually large selective-school share and unusually early tracking ages relative to OECD norms. Converting the three entry points into one at 14 would reduce the number of sorting junctures embedded in current law, though the centralized admissions apparatus and the separate governance track for church/private schools would still need to be reconciled with a single-entry model.
opportunities: A single entry point could simplify oversight of the centralized application process, potentially easing enforcement of uniform admissions criteria across maintainer types
strengths: Directly addresses the early tracking-age problem (Grade 5/7 entry) identified as distinctive relative to OECD norms
Reduces the number of institutional sorting junctures from three to one, shrinking the funnel structure documented in the current system
threats: Existing legal exemptions for church/private schools (as in the Nyíregyháza precedent) could persist as a parallel selection channel even after abolishing gimnázium early-entry tracks, undermining coverage of the reform
weaknesses: Does not by itself resolve the differentiated governance status of church-maintained and private schools, which have historically operated outside standard admissions and anti-discrimination provisions
Centralized Ministry-run admissions process would require significant redesign to accommodate a single national entry point, an administrative shift beyond incremental adjustment
